---
title: "Physics-Informed Neural Networks"
author:
    name: "Emanuele Natale"
    email: emanuele.natale@univ-cotedazur.fr
    affiliation: CNRS, I3S, INRIA, UCA
date: "07 04 2025"
engine: julia
julia:
  exeflags: [
    "--procs=2",
    "--threads=4"
  ]
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    code-line-numbers: false
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 1
    slide-number: true
    overview: true
    scrollable: true
execute:
    echo: true
    freeze: auto
    error: true
---

## Neural Networks

$$
\text{NN}(x) = W_3\sigma_2(W_2\sigma_1(W_1x + b_1) + b_2) + b_3
$$
e.g.

```{julia}
W = [randn(32,10),randn(32,32),randn(5,32)]
b = [zeros(32),zeros(32),zeros(5)]

simpleNN(x) = W[3]*tanh.(W[2]*tanh.(W[1]*x + b[1]) + b[2]) + b[3]
simpleNN(rand(Float32, 10));
```

## Flux

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="30%"}
```{julia}
#| eval: false
]add Flux
```
Then
```{julia}
using Flux
```
If you prefer to namespace commands: `import Flux`
:::
::: {.column width="70%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{julia}
NN2 = Chain(Dense(10,32,tanh),
           Dense(32,32,tanh),
           Dense(32,5))
NN2(rand(Float32, 10))
```
:::
:::
::::

## Layers

Flux written in *pure julia* built on top of **automatic differentiation** libraries

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="65%"}
```{julia}
#| eval: false
struct Dense{F,S<:AbstractArray,T<:AbstractArray}
  W::S
  b::T
  σ::F
end

function Dense(in::Integer, out::Integer,
                σ = identity;
                initW = glorot_uniform,
                initb = zeros)
  return Dense(initW(out, in), initb(out), σ)
end

function (a::Dense)(x::AbstractArray)
  W, b, σ = a.W, a.b, a.σ
  σ.(W*x .+ b)
end
```
:::
::: {.column width="35%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{julia}
f = Dense(32,32,tanh)
f(rand(Float32, 32))
```
:::
:::
::::

## Chain

```{julia}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|1-4|9|6-7|"
struct Chain{T<:Tuple}
  layers::T
  Chain(xs...) = new{typeof(xs)}(xs)
end

applychain(::Tuple{}, x) = x
applychain(fs::Tuple, x) = applychain(tail(fs), first(fs)(x))

(c::Chain)(x) = applychain(c.layers, x)
```

## Note on recursive definition

A function definition lowers to something like

```{julia}
struct ff2 <: Function end
(_::ff2)(x,y) = 2x + y
const ff = ff2()
```

Primitive operation: calls on structs

Why **singleton** types, i.e. where every instance is equivalent?

::: {.fragment}
Ans: we can have the function as compile-time information.
:::

## Zero-cost abstraction

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
Tuples are covariant:
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{julia}
tup = (1.0,1,"1")
typeof(tup)
```
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
`tup = (f,g,h)` has a different type for each of the functions: cannot specialize on the inputs if we use `tup[i]`.
:::

::: {.fragment}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
This can be slow:
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{julia}
#| eval: false
for i in 1:length(tup)
  x = tup[i](x)
end
```
:::
::::
:::

::: {.fragment}
:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
Solution:
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{julia}
#| eval: false
tup[3](tup[2](tup[1](x)))
```
:::
::::
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
How to call chain as if were written out explicitly?
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
Solution: **recursion** with `applychain`.
:::
:::
::::

## Model and loss

Training = find weights minimizing loss function
```{julia}
data = [(rand(Float32,10), ones(5)) for i in 1:100];
NN = Chain(Dense(10,32,tanh),
           Dense(32,32,tanh),
           Dense(32,5))
loss(NN, x, y) = sum(abs2,NN(x) .- y)
loss() = sum(loss(NN,x,y) for (x,y) in data), NN(rand(Float32,10))
loss()
```

::: {.fragment}
What are the weights?
```{julia}
NN[1].weight
```
:::

## Training

```{julia}
#| code-line-numbers: "1|2-4|"
#| output-location: fragment
opt_state = Flux.setup(Adam(0.1), NN)
epochs = 500
for i in 1:epochs
    Flux.train!(loss, NN, data, opt_state)
    i % 100 == 0 && println("Epoch $i: $(loss())")
end
```

::: {.fragment}
Neural networks satisfy the [Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universal_approximation_theorem)
:::

## Why Neural Networks?

Also polynomials can approximate functions (Taylor, Weierstrass...)

::: {.fragment}
[Tensor products](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensor_product) of universal approximators are (higher dimensional) universal approximators
:::
::: {.fragment}
⚠️ $n$ coefficients in each dimension $1,...,d = n^d$ (**curse of dimensionality**)
:::
::: {.fragment}
#### Neural networks

- polynomial of `d` for approximating `d`-dim. functions
- "easy" to compute
- assumptions can be encoded into architecture
:::

## [DifferentialEquations.jl](https://diffeq.sciml.ai/stable/)

- High-performance Julia package for solving diff. eq.s
- Unified interface for ODEs, SDEs, DDEs, PDEs

```{julia}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1|1-5|1-7|1-8|1-9|"
using DifferentialEquations

function diffeq!(du, u, params, t)
    du[1] = -u[1]
end

u0 = [1.0]
tspan = (0.0, 5.0)
prob = ODEProblem(diffeq!, u0, tspan)
sol = solve(prob)
```

## ODE Example: Lotka-Volterra

```{julia}
#| output-location: slide
#| code-line-numbers: "1|1-7|1-9|1-10|1-11|1-13|1-14|"
using DifferentialEquations

function lotka_volterra!(du, u, p, t)
    α, β, δ, γ = p
    du[1] = α*u[1] - β*u[1]*u[2]
    du[2] = δ*u[1]*u[2] - γ*u[2]
end

u0 = [1.0, 1.0]
tspan = (0.0, 10.0)
p = (α = 1.5, β = 1.0, δ = 1.0, γ = 3.0)

prob = ODEProblem(lotka_volterra!, u0, tspan, p)
sol = solve(prob)

using Plots
plot(sol, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Population", label=["Prey" "Predator"])
```

## ODE Example: Replicator Dynamics

```{julia}
#| output-location: slide
function replicator!(du, u, fit_matrix, t)
    fitness = fit_matrix * u
    avg_fitness = u' * fitness
    du[:] = u .* (fitness .- avg_fitness)
end

u0 = [0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]
fit_matrix = rand(4, 4)
tspan = (0.0, 10.0)

prob = ODEProblem(replicator!, u0, tspan, fit_matrix)
sol = solve(prob)

plot(sol, xlabel="Time", ylabel="Frequency",
      label=["Type 1" "Type 2" "Type 3" "Type 4"])
```


## [Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physics-informed_neural_networks)

Scientific Models $\approx$ Differential Equations
$$ F = \frac {\mathbf p}{dt} $$

**Experiments**: info on  $\Delta x$ vs $\Delta y$ for (unknown) law $y = f(x)$

::: {.fragment}
💡 use ANN to improve Scientific Models
:::

## How to use ANN with ODEs?

Problem:

$$u' = f(u,t) \qquad t \in [0,1], u(0)=u_0$$

::: {.fragment}
Assume $NN(t) \approx u(t)$, then $\frac {dNN(t)}{dt} = f(NN(t),t)$

$$\text{loss}(p) = \sum_i \left(\frac{dNN(t_i)}{dt} - f(NN(t_i),t_i) \right)^2$$
:::

## Initial condition

How to satisfy the initial condition $u(0)=u_0$?

::: {.fragment}
#### Solution 1
$$ \small \text{loss}(p) = (NN(0) - u_0)^2 + \sum_i \left(\frac{dNN(t_i)}{dt} - f(NN(t_i),t_i) \right)^2$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
#### Solution 2
Define the scaled ANN
$g(t) = t\cdot NN(t) + u_0$ so that  $g(0) = u_0$ and
$$ \small \text{loss}(p) = \sum_i \left(\frac{dg(t_i)}{dt} - f(g(t_i),t_i) \right)^2$$
:::

## Let's code it (v1)

Example:
$$u' = \cos (2\pi t) , \qquad \text{ where } u(0) = 1.0$$

```{julia}
using Flux
NN = Chain(x->[x],
            Dense(1,32,tanh),
            Dense(32,1),
            first)

g(t,NN) = t*NN(t) + 1f0; # trick
```

## Preparing the training (v1)
We want $g' = \cos (2\pi t)$ so
```{julia}
#| code-line-numbers: "4-5|1,2,7|7-10|"
using Statistics
ϵ = sqrt(eps(Float32))

data = [(t,cos(2π*t)) for t in 0:0.1:1.0]
data = [Float32.(d) for d in data]

loss(NN, t, y) = abs2(((g(t+ϵ,NN) - g(t,NN))/ϵ) - y)
loss() = mean(loss(NN, t, y) for (t,y) in data)

loss()
```

## Training the PINN (v1)

```{julia}
opt = Flux.setup(Adam(), NN)
epochs = 1000
for i in 1:epochs
  i % 100 == 0 && println("Epoch $i: $(loss())")
  Flux.train!(loss, NN, data, opt)
end
```

## Let's code it again (v2)

Example:
$$u' = \cos (2\pi t) , \quad \text{ where } u(0) = 1.0$$

```{julia}
#| output-location: column
#| code-line-numbers: "11|"
using Flux
NN = Chain(x->[x],
            Dense(1,32,tanh),
            Dense(32,1),
            first)

struct PINN{T <: Chain}
  NN::T
end

(g2::PINN)(t) = g2.NN(t)*t + 1f0
Flux.@layer PINN

g2 = PINN(NN)
```

## Preparing the training (v2)

We want $g' = \cos (2\pi t)$ so
```{julia}
#| code-line-numbers: "4-5|1,2,7|7-10|"
using Statistics
ϵ = sqrt(eps(Float32))

data = [(t,cos(2π*t)) for t in 0:0.1:1.0]
data = [Float32.(d) for d in data]

loss(g2, t, y) = abs2(((g2(t+ϵ) - g2(t))/ϵ) - y)

loss() = mean(loss(g2, t, y) for (t,y) in data)
loss()
```

## Training the PINN (v2)

```{julia}
opt = Flux.setup(Adam(), g2)
epochs = 1000
for i in 1:epochs
  i % 100 == 0 && println("Epoch $i: $(loss())")
  Flux.train!(loss, g2, data, opt)
end
```

## Testing the PINN

By definition
$$
\int g' = \int \cos 2\pi t = C + \frac{\sin 2\pi t}{2\pi}
$$

```{julia}
#| output-location: slide
t = Float32.(0:0.001:1.0)
plot(t,g2.(t),label="NN")
plot!(t, 1.0 .+ sin.(2π.*t)/2π, label = "True Solution")
```

## Example: Harmonic-Oscillator

Physical laws can be *encoded* in the loss function

Measurements of (position,force) of real 1-d spring pushing and pulling against wall
$$F(x) = \frac {d^2x}{dt^2} = -kx + 0.1\sin(x)$$

```{julia}
#| output-location: slide
k = 1.0
x0, v0 = 1.0, 0.0
tspan = (0.0, 10.0)
force(dx,x,k,t) = -k*x + 0.1sin(x)
prob = SecondOrderODEProblem(force,v0,x0,tspan,k)
sol = solve(prob)
plot(sol,label=["Velocity" "Position"])
```

## Data: few measurements

```{julia}
#| output-location: slide
t = 0:3.3:10

plot_t = 0:0.01:10
data_plot = sol(plot_t)
pos_plot = Float32.([state[2] for state in data_plot])
force_plot = [force(state[1],state[2],k,t) for state in data_plot]

dataset = sol(t)
pos_data = Float32.([state[2] for state in dataset])
force_data = Float32.([force(state[1],state[2],k,t) for state in dataset])

plot(plot_t,force_plot,xlabel="t",label="True Force")
scatter!(t,force_data,label="Force Measurements")
```

## Using only the data

```{julia}
NNForce = Chain(x -> [x],
           Dense(1,32,tanh),
           Dense(32,1),
           first)
```

Loss should match force at (position,force) in  dataset:
```{julia}
loss(NNForce) = sum(abs2, NNForce(pos_data[i]) - force_data[i]
                          for i in 1:length(pos_data))
loss(NNForce)
```

## Training the PINN

```{julia}
opt = Flux.setup(Adam(), NNForce)
data = Iterators.repeated((), 1)
epochs = 1000
for i in 1:epochs
  i % 100 == 0 && println("Epoch $i: $(loss(NNForce))")
  Flux.train!(loss, NNForce, data, opt)
end
```

## Checking what we learned

```{julia}
learned_force_plot = NNForce.(pos_plot)
plot(plot_t,force_plot,xlabel="t",label="True Force")
plot!(plot_t,learned_force_plot,label="Predicted Force")
scatter!(t,force_data,label="Force Measurements")
```

## Guessing some Physics

[Hooke's law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hooke's_law):
$$ F(x) = -kx $$

```{julia}
#| output-location: slide
force2(dx,x,k,t) = -k*x
prob_simplified = SecondOrderODEProblem(force2,v0,x0,tspan,k)
sol_simplified = solve(prob_simplified)

plot(sol,label=["Velocity" "Position"])
plot!(sol_simplified,label=["Velocity Simplified" "Position Simplified"])
```

## Adding the Physics

```{julia}
rand_pos = Float32.([2rand()-1 for i in 1:100])
loss_ode(NNForce) = sum(abs2,NNForce(x) - (-k*x) for x in rand_pos)
loss_ode(NNForce)
```

When would this term be zero?

::: {.fragment}
```{julia}
λ = 0.1
comb_loss(NNForce) = loss(NNForce) + λ*loss_ode(NNForce)
```
$λ$ weights the physics assumption
:::

## Data + Physics

```{julia}
opt = Flux.setup(Adam(), NNForce)
data = Iterators.repeated((), 1)
for i in 1:epochs
  i % 100 == 0 && println("Epoch $i: $(loss(NNForce))")
  Flux.train!(comb_loss, NNForce, data, opt)
end
```

```{julia}
#| output-location: slide
learned_force_plot = NNForce.(pos_plot)
plot(plot_t,force_plot,xlabel="t",label="True Force")
plot!(plot_t,learned_force_plot,label="Predicted Force")
scatter!(t,force_data,label="Force Measurements")
```
